nodes.rationale: Identifying and mitigating disruption, delay, and price risks in supply, e.g., via multiple sourcing and flexible suppliers.

nodes.label: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 15: Sourcing Decisions in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Over half of sales for most manufacturers, increasingly dominated by purchased parts as firms reduce vertical integration.

nodes.label: Transportation Strategy Alignment
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: Transportation in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Aligning transportation decisions with competitive strategy and evaluating the function on total cost and responsiveness, not transport cost alone.

nodes.label: Transportation Network Flexibility
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: Transportation in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Designing transportation networks that account for demand and capacity uncertainty by including flexible, if costlier, modes.

nodes.label: Congestion Pricing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 14: Transportation in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Pricing infrastructure use so users internalize the network cost they impose, preventing overutilization and congestion.

nodes.label: Pricing and Revenue Management
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Use of differential pricing to better match supply and demand and maximize total supply chain profit from existing assets.

nodes.label: Customer Segmentation
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Separating customers into distinct segments with different willingness to pay using product or service attributes (e.g., advance booking, lead time).

nodes.label: Demand Curve
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Relationship between price and quantity demanded (often modeled linearly as d = A - Bp) used to set profit-maximizing prices.

nodes.label: Capacity Allocation Under Uncertainty
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Deciding how much capacity to reserve for higher-price segments so expected marginal revenue equals the lower-price; trades off spoilage vs spill.

nodes.label: Spoilage and Spill
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Spoilage is wasting capacity reserved for high-price demand that does not arrive; spill is turning away high-price buyers because capacity was committed to low-price buyers.

nodes.label: Perishable Assets
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Any asset that loses value over time, including fashion apparel, electronics, and unused production, transportation, or storage capacity.

nodes.label: Off-Peak Discounting
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Charging lower prices in off-peak periods to shift or attract demand away from seasonal peaks, reducing peak capacity cost.

nodes.label: Seasonal Peak Demand
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Distinct seasonal surges in demand (e.g., December retail) that strain capacity and create revenue management opportunities via pricing.

nodes.label: Bulk and Spot Contracts
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Portfolio decision of buying assets via low-price long-term bulk contracts that may be wasted versus higher-price spot market purchases never wasted.

nodes.label: Price Optimization
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Solving constrained or unconstrained optimization problems (often in Excel Solver) to set prices that maximize profit across segments and periods.

nodes.label: Strategic Customer Behavior
nodes.source_location: Chapter 16: Pricing and Revenue Management in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Customers anticipating future price drops and delaying purchases, which can erode the benefit of frequent discounting on high-value goods.

nodes.label: Information Technology in a Supply Chain
nodes.source_location: Chapter 17: Information Technology in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Hardware, software, and people that gather, analyze, and execute on information to improve supply chain decision making and performance.
nodes.norm_label: information technology in a supply chain

nodes.label: Supply Chain Visibility
nodes.source_location: Chapter 17: Information Technology in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Information-enabled awareness of customer demand, inventory, and supply status that lets managers make better supply chain decisions.

nodes.label: Transaction Management Foundation (TMF)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 17: Information Technology in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Base IT layer (e.g., ERP) handling basic transactions and infrastructure that supports the CRM, ISCM, and SRM macro processes.

nodes.label: Software as a Service (SaaS)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 17: Information Technology in a Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Cloud-based delivery model for supply chain software, an emerging trend in the future of supply chain IT versus buying and hosting software.

nodes.label: Tragedy of the Commons
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Hardin's concept where sustainability actions impose local costs but yield global benefits, so external mandates or incentives are needed to encourage them.

nodes.label: Sustainability Metrics
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Four core environmental categories tracked across the supply chain: energy consumption, water consumption, greenhouse gas emissions, and waste generation.

nodes.label: Greenhouse Gas Protocol (Scope 1/2/3)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Standard defining Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (purchased energy), and Scope 3 (other indirect supply chain) emissions for measurement and reporting.

nodes.label: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Method to assess environmental impacts associated with a product's life from cradle to grave to reduce harmful landfill inventory and unlock unused value.

nodes.label: Cradle-to-Cradle Design
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: McDonough and Braungart design philosophy where end-of-life products become nutrients or high-quality raw materials rather than waste.

nodes.label: Landfill Inventory
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Product discarded into landfills representing the most damaging supply chain waste, locking away materials and energy and causing harm.

nodes.label: Absolute vs Relative Sustainability Measures
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Reporting total resource use (absolute) versus per-unit-of-output use (relative); firms should report both to capture true performance.

nodes.label: Emission Tax and Cap-and-Trade
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Policy mechanisms charging for or capping emissions to reduce greenhouse gases; effectiveness depends on the charge level and global coordination.

nodes.label: Sustainability Across Supply Chain Drivers
nodes.source_location: Chapter 18: Sustainability and the Supply Chain
nodes.rationale: Identifying improvement opportunities by matching the four environmental categories against facilities, inventory, transportation, sourcing, information, and pricing.

nodes.label: Heizer, Render & Munson: Operations Management (12th ed.)
nodes.author: Jay Heizer; Barry Render; Chuck Munson
nodes.rationale: Operations management textbook: strategy, project mgmt, quality/SPC, process & layout design, location, scheduling.
nodes.norm_label: heizer, render & munson: operations management (12th ed.)

nodes.label: Single-Factor Productivity
nodes.rationale: A productivity measure that indicates the ratio of total output to a single input, such as labor hours.

nodes.label: Multifactor Productivity
nodes.rationale: A productivity measure (also called total factor productivity) that combines multiple inputs (labor, material, energy, capital) into a single ratio with output.

nodes.label: Productivity Variables
nodes.rationale: The three factors critical to productivity improvement: labor, capital, and management.

nodes.label: Operations Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2 Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: An organization's approach to using its operations resources to support and achieve a sustainable competitive advantage through its mission and strategy.

nodes.label: Competing on Differentiation
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2 Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Distinguishing the offerings of an organization in a way the customer perceives as adding value (uniqueness), going beyond physical characteristics to encompass the entire experience.

nodes.label: Competing on Cost
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2 Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Providing the maximum value as perceived by the customer at the lowest cost, often through high facility utilization and low-cost leadership.

nodes.label: Competing on Response
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2 Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: A set of values related to rapid, flexible, and reliable performance; the ability to respond quickly and dependably to customer needs and market changes.

nodes.label: Ten Strategic OM Decisions
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2 Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: The ten decision areas of operations management: goods/service design, quality, process, location, layout, human resources, supply chain, inventory, scheduling, and maintenance.

nodes.label: Key Success Factors
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2 Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Activities or factors that are key to achieving competitive advantage; a firm must do them exceptionally well to succeed in its market.

nodes.label: Theory of Comparative Advantage
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2 Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Economic principle stating that a country (or firm) benefits by specializing in and exporting what it can produce relatively most efficiently, underpinning outsourcing decisions.

nodes.label: Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
nodes.rationale: A hierarchical decomposition of a project into its major subcomponents, tasks, subtasks, and work packages to define the total scope of work.

nodes.label: Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)
nodes.rationale: A project management network technique that uses three time estimates (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic) per activity to handle variability and probabilistic completion times.

nodes.label: Critical Path Method (CPM)
nodes.rationale: A project management network technique that models project activities and their dependencies to determine the longest path of activities and minimum project duration.

nodes.label: Critical Path
nodes.rationale: The longest time path through a project network; any delay to an activity on this path delays the entire project. Its activities have zero slack.

nodes.label: Project Network Diagram
nodes.rationale: A graphical depiction of project activities and their precedence relationships, drawn as activity-on-node (AON) or activity-on-arrow (AOA).

nodes.label: Forward Pass
nodes.rationale: A process that identifies the earliest start (ES) and earliest finish (EF) times for each activity by moving through the network from start to end.

nodes.label: Backward Pass
nodes.rationale: A process that identifies the latest start (LS) and latest finish (LF) times for each activity by moving backward through the network from end to start.

nodes.label: Slack Time
nodes.rationale: The free time an activity has; the amount by which an activity can be delayed without delaying the entire project. Computed as LS minus ES (or LF minus EF).

nodes.label: Three Time Estimates (PERT)
nodes.rationale: PERT's optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic activity-duration estimates used to compute expected time and variance via a beta distribution.

nodes.label: Project Crashing
nodes.rationale: Shortening project duration by reducing the time of one or more critical activities at additional cost, prioritizing the lowest crash-cost-per-period activities on the critical path.

nodes.label: Cost-Time Trade-Off
nodes.rationale: The relationship between the cost of shortening an activity (crash cost) and the time saved, used to find the least-cost way to reduce total project duration.

nodes.label: Gantt Chart
nodes.rationale: A planning and control bar chart that shows project or production activities against a time scale, used for scheduling and tracking progress.

nodes.label: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: The selection, definition, and development of products and services, translating customer needs into producible specifications that support competitive advantage.

nodes.label: Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A process for determining customer requirements (customer wants) and translating them into target product design attributes, often using the House of Quality matrix.

nodes.label: House of Quality
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A matrix tool within QFD that relates customer wants to the firm's technical attributes (the hows), making explicit the relationships and trade-offs in design.

nodes.label: Robust Design
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A design that can be produced to requirements even with unfavorable conditions in the production process, reducing sensitivity to variation (a Taguchi concept).

nodes.label: Modular Design
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A design in which products are built from easily segmented standardized components (modules), enabling flexibility, easier service, and mass customization.

nodes.label: Computer-Aided Design (CAD)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Interactive use of computers to develop and document a product design, often linked to computer-aided manufacturing (CAM) for production.

nodes.label: Value Engineering
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A review of products during production aimed at improving the product's value by reducing cost or enhancing function without sacrificing performance.

nodes.label: Group Technology
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A coding system that groups components by similar characteristics so that design and manufacturing efforts can be standardized and shared.

nodes.label: Make-or-Buy Decision
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: The choice between producing a component internally or purchasing it from an outside supplier, central to product definition and sourcing strategy.

nodes.label: Decision Trees in Product Design
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A graphical analysis tool that maps sequential design decisions and uncertain outcomes, evaluated using expected monetary value (EMV).

nodes.label: Process Strategy
nodes.rationale: An organization's approach to transforming resources into goods and services, choosing among process focus, repetitive focus, product focus, and mass customization.

nodes.label: Service Design
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5 Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: The design of services accounting for their intangibility and customer interaction, using tools such as process-chain-network (PCN) analysis and service blueprinting.

nodes.label: Differentiation Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Competing by distinguishing offerings (better or different) so customers perceive added value beyond physical and service attributes.

nodes.label: Experience Differentiation
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Engaging the customer's five senses so they experience the product or service, a service-sector form of differentiation.

nodes.label: Low-Cost Leadership
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Competing by achieving maximum value as perceived by the customer through relentless cost reduction across the ten OM decisions.

nodes.label: Response Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Competing through flexible, reliable, and quick performance in product development, scheduling, and delivery.

nodes.label: Flexible Response
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: The ability to match changes in a marketplace where design innovations and volumes fluctuate substantially.

nodes.label: Sustainable Competitive Advantage
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: A competitive advantage built by integrating and reinforcing core competencies and key success factors so rivals cannot easily copy it.

nodes.label: Mission
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: The purpose or rationale for an organization's existence that provides boundaries and focus and guides strategy development.

nodes.label: Activity Map
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: A graphical link of competitive advantage, key success factors, and the supporting activities that reinforce them.

nodes.label: Value-Chain Analysis
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Porter's method of identifying elements in the product/service chain that uniquely add value and offer competitive advantage.

nodes.label: Porter's Five Forces Model
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: A method of analyzing five competitive forces: immediate rivals, potential entrants, customers, suppliers, and substitute products.

nodes.label: Resources View
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Evaluating financial, physical, human, and technological resources to ensure a strategy is compatible with what the firm has.

nodes.label: Product Life Cycle Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Adapting OM strategy and issues as products move through introduction, growth, maturity, and decline phases.

nodes.label: Contract Manufacturing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Subcontracting production activities to an external manufacturer on a continuing basis, common from computers to automobiles.

nodes.label: Reshoring
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: The return of business activity to its originating country, often after offshore costs rise or quality problems emerge.

nodes.label: Outsourcing Risk Management
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Realistic analysis of uncertainty (delivery, quality, logistics, political backlash) to minimize the impact of outsourcing failures.

nodes.label: Factor-Rating Method
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: An objective technique that scores alternatives on weighted criteria to evaluate outsource providers and countries.

nodes.label: Global Operations Strategy Options
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Four strategies (international, multidomestic, global, transnational) positioned on cost reduction versus local responsiveness.

nodes.label: International Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Penetrating global markets using exports and licenses, with little local responsiveness and little cost advantage.

nodes.label: Multidomestic Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Decentralizing operating decisions to each country to maximize local responsiveness, with little cost advantage.

nodes.label: Global Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Centralizing operating decisions with headquarters coordinating standardization and learning across plants for economies of scale.

nodes.label: Transnational Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Combining global-scale efficiencies with local responsiveness by letting core competence reside anywhere across worldwide operations.

nodes.label: Local Responsiveness
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Quick response and differentiation tailored to the needs of a particular local market; one axis of global strategy choice.

nodes.label: Multinational Corporation (MNC)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: A firm with extensive international business that buys resources and sells goods/services across many countries.

nodes.label: Globalization of Operations
nodes.source_location: Chapter 2: Operations Strategy in a Global Environment
nodes.rationale: Reasons firms internationalize operations: reduce costs, improve supply chain, deliver better goods/services, understand markets, learn, and attract talent.

nodes.label: Project Planning
nodes.rationale: First project phase: goal setting, defining the project, and organizing the team to ensure proper management and attention.

nodes.label: Project Scheduling
nodes.rationale: Phase that relates people, money, and supplies to activities and sequences activities to identify precedence relationships and bottlenecks.

nodes.label: Project Controlling
nodes.rationale: Phase that monitors resources, costs, quality, and budgets and revises plans, shifting resources to meet time and cost demands.

nodes.label: Activity-on-Node (AON)
nodes.rationale: Network diagram convention in which nodes designate activities and arrows show precedence relationships.

nodes.label: Activity-on-Arrow (AOA)
nodes.rationale: Network diagram convention in which arrows designate activities and nodes represent start/finish events.

nodes.label: Dummy Activity
nodes.rationale: Zero-time activity inserted into a network to preserve correct logical precedence relationships among real activities.

nodes.label: PERT Three Time Estimates
nodes.rationale: Optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic activity times combined under a beta distribution to compute expected time and variance.

nodes.label: Variability in Activity Times
nodes.rationale: PERT modeling of uncertainty using activity variance and project variance to estimate the probability of meeting a due date.

nodes.label: Project Organization
nodes.rationale: Temporary organizational structure formed to ensure a project receives proper cross-functional management and attention.

nodes.label: Forecasting
nodes.rationale: The art and science of predicting future events such as demand, providing the only estimate of demand until actual demand is known.

nodes.label: Forecasting Time Horizons
nodes.rationale: Classification of forecasts as short-, medium-, or long-range, each driving different operations decisions and using different methods.

nodes.label: Demand-Driven Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Forecasting that rapidly identifies and tracks real customer demand using point-of-sale and retailer data rather than only history.

nodes.label: Economic Forecast
nodes.rationale: Forecast of business-cycle planning indicators such as inflation, money supply, and housing starts used for medium- to long-range plans.

nodes.label: Technological Forecast
nodes.rationale: Long-term forecast of rates of technological progress that can spawn new products requiring new plants and equipment.

nodes.label: Strategic Importance of Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Forecast accuracy is critical to supply-chain management, human resources, and capacity decisions across the firm.

nodes.label: Product Life Cycle and Forecasting
nodes.rationale: Forecasts are influenced by a product's life-cycle stage (introduction, growth, maturity, decline), changing method and accuracy.

nodes.label: Product Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Links product decisions to investment, market share and life cycle, defining the product line to win competitive advantage via differentiation, low cost, or rapid response.

nodes.label: Product Decision
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: The selection, definition, and design of products; one of the ten strategic OM decisions with major implications across the operations function.

nodes.label: Product-by-Value Analysis
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Lists products in descending order of their individual dollar contribution to the firm to apply the Pareto principle and focus resources on the critical few.

nodes.label: Product Development System
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A staged process in which product options pass through screening and evaluation criteria with continuous feedback, linking R&D, engineering, marketing, and finance.

nodes.label: Concurrent Engineering
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Simultaneous performance of the various stages of product development, facilitated by cross-functional teams to speed time to market.

nodes.label: Product Development Team
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Cross-functional teams charged with moving from market requirements to product success, including marketability, manufacturability, and serviceability.

nodes.label: Manufacturability and Value Engineering
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Preproduction activities improving design and specifications to reduce cost and complexity while meeting functional requirements; typically cuts cost 15-70%.

nodes.label: Value Analysis
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A review of successful products during production that seeks a better, cheaper, or lower-environmental-impact product, applied after value engineering.

nodes.label: Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: CAD-extension software that lets designers evaluate the effect of a design on manufacturing and assembly while the product is still being designed.

nodes.label: Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Use of information technology and specialized programs to direct and control manufacturing equipment; combined with CAD yields CAD/CAM.

nodes.label: 3-D Printing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: An extension of CAD that builds prototypes and small lots by laying down successive thin layers of material, speeding development and customization.

nodes.label: Virtual Reality Technology
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A visual form of communication, rooted in CAD, in which images substitute for reality and allow interactive testing of layouts and designs before build.

nodes.label: Standard for the Exchange of Product Data (STEP)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: ISO 10303 standard providing a format that lets 3-D product data be expressed and transmitted electronically and exchanged internationally.
nodes.norm_label: standard for the exchange of product data (step)

nodes.label: Sustainability and Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Designing to meet present needs without compromising future generations, with LCA as a formal evaluation of a product's environmental impact.

nodes.label: Product Development Continuum
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A spectrum of development strategies from internal (new products, enhancements, migrations on product platforms) to external (purchase, joint ventures, alliances).

nodes.label: Product Platform
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A shared base design migrated across many models (e.g. VW MQB chassis, Boeing 737) to speed development while reducing cost and risk.

nodes.label: Joint Venture
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Combined ownership, usually between two firms, forming a new entity to pursue specific product opportunities with known and equitably shared risks.

nodes.label: Strategic Alliance
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Cooperative agreements that let firms remain independent while combining complementary strengths to develop products under significant unknowns and risk.

nodes.label: Bill of Material (BOM)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A list of the hierarchy of components, their description, and the quantity of each required to make one unit of a product; portion-control standards in food service.

nodes.label: Engineering Drawing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A drawing showing the dimensions, tolerances, materials, and finishes of a component; referenced as an item on the bill of material.

nodes.label: Assembly Drawing
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: An exploded, usually isometric, view of a product showing the relative locations of components to indicate how the unit is assembled.

nodes.label: Assembly Chart
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A schematic graphic identifying how manufactured and purchased components flow into subassemblies and ultimately into the final product.

nodes.label: Route Sheet
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A listing of the operations necessary to produce a component with the specified material; with methods and labor standards it becomes a process sheet.

nodes.label: Engineering Change Notice (ECN)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A correction or modification of an engineering drawing or bill of material that changes some aspect of a product's definition or documentation.

nodes.label: Configuration Management
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: A system by which a product's planned and changing configurations are accurately identified and change is controlled and documented.

nodes.label: Product Life-Cycle Management (PLM)
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: An umbrella of software tying together product design and manufacture phases (CAD/CAM, DFMA, routing, materials, layout, maintenance) on consistent data.

nodes.label: Process-Chain-Network (PCN) Analysis
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Sampson's analysis of how processes can be designed across direct, surrogate, and independent regions to optimize firm-customer interaction.

nodes.label: Service Efficiency and Customization Strategy
nodes.source_location: Chapter 5: Design of Goods and Services
nodes.rationale: Tactics to raise service productivity by limiting options, delaying customization, and modularizing the service to constrain customer interaction.

nodes.label: Shared Value
nodes.rationale: Policies and practices that enhance an organization's competitiveness while advancing economic and social conditions in the communities where it operates.

nodes.label: ISO 14000
nodes.rationale: ISO family of guidelines for sustainable development; ISO 14001 addresses environmental management systems.

nodes.label: Total Quality Management (TQM)
nodes.rationale: Management of an entire organization, supplier to customer, so it excels in all aspects of products and services important to the customer.

nodes.label: PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)
nodes.rationale: Shewhart/Deming circular continuous-improvement model of plan, do, check, and act.

nodes.label: Kaizen
nodes.rationale: Japanese term for the ongoing process of unending improvement: continually setting and achieving ever-higher goals.

nodes.label: DMAIC Improvement Model
nodes.rationale: Five-step Six Sigma process-improvement model: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control.

nodes.label: Quality Circle
nodes.rationale: A group of employees meeting regularly with a facilitator to solve work-related problems in their work area.

nodes.label: Taguchi Concepts
nodes.rationale: Genichi Taguchi's quality concepts: quality robustness, target-oriented quality, and the quality loss function, removing the effects of adverse conditions.

nodes.label: Quality Robustness
nodes.rationale: Products built to perform uniformly and consistently despite adverse manufacturing and environmental conditions.

nodes.label: Target-Oriented Quality
nodes.rationale: Philosophy of continuous improvement to bring a product exactly on its target value rather than merely within specification limits.

nodes.label: Quality Loss Function (QLF)
nodes.rationale: A quadratic function estimating all costs of poor quality, showing how cost rises as output deviates from the target value.

nodes.label: Cost of Quality (COQ)
nodes.rationale: The cost of doing things wrong: prevention, appraisal, internal failure, and external failure costs.

nodes.label: ISO 9000
nodes.rationale: Internationally recognized set of quality-management standards developed by the ISO, emphasizing documented procedures and continual improvement.

nodes.label: Seven Tools of TQM
nodes.rationale: Check sheets, scatter diagrams, cause-and-effect diagrams, Pareto charts, flowcharts, histograms, and statistical process control charts.

nodes.label: Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa/Fish-Bone)
nodes.rationale: A schematic fish-bone technique using the 4Ms (material, machinery, manpower, methods) to discover possible locations of quality problems.

nodes.label: Pareto Chart
nodes.rationale: A graphic that classifies problems by importance (the 80-20 rule) to focus problem-solving on the few critical causes.

nodes.label: Inspection
nodes.rationale: A means of ensuring an operation is producing at the expected quality level; complements but cannot substitute for building quality in.

nodes.label: Source Inspection
nodes.rationale: Controlling or monitoring quality at the point of production or purchase, with each step treating the next as its customer.

nodes.label: Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing)
nodes.rationale: A foolproof device or technique that ensures production of a good unit every time and provides quick feedback of problems.

nodes.label: Checklist
nodes.rationale: A type of poka-yoke listing the steps needed to ensure consistency and completeness in carrying out a task.

nodes.label: Service Recovery
nodes.rationale: Training and empowering frontline workers to immediately solve a customer problem when service fails.

nodes.label: SERVQUAL
nodes.rationale: A measurement scale for service quality comparing customer expectations with perceived performance across service-quality determinants.

nodes.label: Statistical Process Control (SPC)
nodes.rationale: Application of statistical techniques to monitor standards and take corrective action while a product or service is being produced.

nodes.label: Control Chart
nodes.rationale: A graphical presentation of process data over time with control limits, used to distinguish natural from assignable variation.

nodes.label: Natural (Common-Cause) Variation
nodes.rationale: Inherent variability expected in every process; when it is the only source, the process is in statistical control.

nodes.label: Assignable (Special-Cause) Variation
nodes.rationale: Variation traceable to a specific cause (machine wear, untrained workers, new materials) that must be identified and eliminated.

nodes.label: Central Limit Theorem
nodes.rationale: Theoretical foundation for x-bar charts: sample means tend toward a normal distribution regardless of the population distribution.

nodes.label: x-bar Chart
nodes.rationale: A control chart for variables that signals when the central tendency (mean) of a production process has changed.

nodes.label: R-Chart (Range Chart)
nodes.rationale: A control chart tracking the range within samples to detect gains or losses in process dispersion.

nodes.label: p-Chart and c-Chart (Attribute Charts)
nodes.rationale: Control charts for attributes: p-charts monitor the fraction defective; c-charts monitor the number of defects per unit.

nodes.label: Process Capability (Cp, Cpk)
nodes.rationale: The ability of a process to meet design specifications, quantified by the indices Cp and Cpk.

nodes.label: Acceptance Sampling
nodes.rationale: Inspecting a random sample of a lot of material or products to accept or reject the entire lot.

nodes.label: Process Focus
nodes.rationale: A production facility organized around processes (job shops) to facilitate low-volume, high-variety, intermittent production with high product flexibility.

nodes.label: Repetitive Focus
nodes.rationale: A product-oriented assembly-line process that uses pre-prepared modules, blending product-focus economics with quasi-custom output.

nodes.label: Product Focus
nodes.rationale: A facility organized around products; a high-volume, low-variety continuous process with high fixed cost and low variable cost rewarding high utilization.

nodes.label: Intermittent Process
nodes.rationale: A process designed to perform a variety of activities with frequent changes, characteristic of process-focused job shops.

nodes.label: Continuous Process
nodes.rationale: A long, uninterrupted production run characteristic of product-focused facilities making standardized goods such as paper, beer, or chips.

nodes.label: Process Crossover Point
nodes.rationale: The volume (point of indifference) at which the total cost of one process alternative equals another, used to select among competing processes or technologies.

nodes.label: Process Analysis and Design
nodes.rationale: Examination and design of a process using tools such as flowcharts, time-function maps, process charts, value-stream maps, and service blueprints.

nodes.label: Flowchart
nodes.rationale: A drawing used to analyze the movement of people, material, or product through a process.

nodes.label: Time-Function Mapping
nodes.rationale: A flowchart with time added on the horizontal axis (also called process mapping) to expose value-added steps and delays.

nodes.label: Value-Stream Mapping
nodes.rationale: A lean tool that extends process analysis upstream to suppliers, mapping material and information flow across the value stream to find waste.

nodes.label: Service Blueprinting
nodes.rationale: A process-analysis technique for high-service-content products that focuses on the customer and the moments of interaction with the service.

nodes.label: Process Redesign
nodes.rationale: The fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes to achieve improvements in cost, quality, speed, or service.

nodes.label: Production Technology
nodes.rationale: Technologies (machine controls, automation, robots, sensors, vision systems) that enhance production and maintain tolerances and quality.

nodes.label: Capacity
nodes.rationale: The throughput, or the number of units a facility can hold, receive, store, or produce in a given time.

nodes.label: Design Capacity
nodes.rationale: The maximum theoretical output of a system in a given period under ideal conditions.

nodes.label: Effective Capacity
nodes.rationale: The capacity a firm expects to achieve given its product mix, scheduling, maintenance, and quality standards (less than design capacity).

nodes.label: Capacity Efficiency
nodes.rationale: Actual output as a percent of effective capacity, measuring how well effective capacity is used.

nodes.label: Capacity Planning
nodes.rationale: Determining the facility size and capacity additions needed to achieve high utilization and return on investment over the planning horizon.

nodes.label: Bottleneck Time
nodes.rationale: The process time of the longest (slowest) process in a system; system capacity is the inverse of the bottleneck time.

nodes.label: Throughput Time
nodes.rationale: The total time required for a unit to move through a production system from start to end assuming no waiting (empty-system behavior).

nodes.label: Break-Even Analysis
nodes.rationale: Finding the point in dollars and units at which total cost equals total revenue, used to evaluate capacity and process profitability.

nodes.label: Break-Even Point
nodes.rationale: The volume at which total revenue equals total cost (fixed plus variable); production above this point yields profit.

nodes.label: Fixed and Variable Costs
nodes.rationale: Cost categories underlying break-even and process selection: fixed costs are independent of volume while variable costs rise with units produced.

nodes.label: Decision Analysis
nodes.rationale: A structured approach to choosing among capacity and investment alternatives under uncertainty, here applied through expected monetary value.

nodes.label: Expected Monetary Value (EMV)
nodes.rationale: The probability-weighted payoff of a decision alternative; selecting the alternative with the highest EMV is a decision criterion under uncertainty in capacity decisions.

nodes.label: Present Value
nodes.rationale: The current worth of a future cash flow discounted at an interest rate, the building block of net present value analysis.

nodes.label: Jacobs & Chase: Operations and Supply Chain Management (15th ed.)
nodes.author: F. Robert Jacobs; Richard B. Chase
nodes.rationale: McGraw-Hill 2018 comprehensive OSCM textbook; scanned for methods new to Linchpin - added queuing, scheduling/assignment, earned value, learning curve, lean sizing, capacity cushion, acceptance sampling, and DEA.
nodes.norm_label: jacobs & chase: operations and supply chain management (15th ed.)

nodes.label: Waiting-Line / Queuing Analysis
nodes.rationale: Closed-form congestion models turning arrival rate (lambda) and service rate (mu) into queue length and wait time for capacity/staffing decisions. Implemented in src/queuing.py.

nodes.label: M/M/1 Queue
nodes.rationale: Single-server Poisson/exponential queue: Lq=rho^2/(1-rho), Wq=Lq/lambda. Implemented in src/queuing.py.

nodes.label: M/M/c Queue (Erlang-C)
nodes.rationale: Multi-server waiting-line model with the Erlang-C probability of wait. Implemented in src/queuing.py.

nodes.label: M/D/1 Constant-Service Queue
nodes.rationale: Constant service time halves the M/M/1 queue length. Implemented in src/queuing.py.

nodes.label: Kingman / G/G/c Wait Approximation
nodes.rationale: Distribution-free wait approximation: wait scales with variability (Ca^2+Cs^2)/2 and explodes as utilization -> 1. Implemented in src/queuing.py.

nodes.label: Finite-Source (Machine-Repair) Queue
nodes.rationale: Finite-population queue (N units, S repairers) solved by the birth-death recursion. Implemented in src/queuing.py.

nodes.label: Queuing Server-Count Cost Optimization
nodes.rationale: Pick the server count minimizing (in-system cost) + (server cost). Implemented in src/queuing.py.

nodes.label: Johnson's Rule (Two-Machine Flow Shop)
nodes.rationale: Optimal sequence minimizing makespan through two machines in series. Implemented in src/scheduling.py.

nodes.label: Assignment Method (Hungarian Algorithm)
nodes.rationale: Optimal one-to-one allocation (job->machine, SKU->DC, shipment->lane) minimizing total cost. Implemented in src/scheduling.py.

nodes.label: Dispatching Rules (SPT/EDD/FCFS/LPT)
nodes.rationale: Single-machine priority sequencing; SPT minimizes mean flow time, EDD minimizes max lateness. Implemented in src/scheduling.py.

nodes.label: First-Hour Shift Scheduling
nodes.rationale: Greedy roster covering a time-varying hourly requirement with fixed-length shifts. Implemented in src/scheduling.py.

nodes.label: Earned Value Management (SPI/CPI)
nodes.rationale: Project cost/schedule control: SV, CV, SPI=BCWP/BCWS, CPI=BCWP/AC. Implemented in src/earned_value.py.

nodes.label: Learning Curve (Yx = K x^n)
nodes.rationale: Unit cost/time as cumulative output doubles; n=ln(rate)/ln(2). For cost-down forecasting/quoting. Implemented in src/learning_curve.py.

nodes.label: Kanban Card-Set Sizing
nodes.rationale: Pull-loop container count k = D*L*(1+S)/C (WIP cap = k*C). Implemented in src/kanban.py.

nodes.label: Takt Time
nodes.rationale: Demand pace = available work time / required units; cycle time above takt is a shortfall. Implemented in src/kanban.py.

nodes.label: Capacity Cushion
nodes.rationale: Excess-capacity fraction (cap-demand)/demand; utilization = 1/(1+cushion). Implemented in src/capacity_planning.py.

nodes.label: Single Acceptance Sampling Plan Design
nodes.rationale: Derive inspect-n / accept-c from AQL/LTPD and producer/consumer risk via the binomial OC curve. Implemented in src/acceptance_sampling.py.

nodes.label: 100%-Inspection Break-Even Rule
nodes.rationale: Inspect all iff unit inspection cost < defect rate * downstream failure cost. Implemented in src/acceptance_sampling.py.

nodes.label: Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA)
nodes.rationale: LP efficiency frontier rating peer units on multi-input/multi-output without preset weights. Implemented in src/dea.py.

nodes.label: Assembly-Line Balancing
nodes.rationale: Assign precedence-constrained tasks to stations: cycle time, theoretical min stations, efficiency.

nodes.label: Load-Distance Layout Optimization
nodes.rationale: Minimize sum(load*distance) over department arrangements (a quadratic-assignment layout cost).

nodes.label: Depreciation Schedule Methods (SL/SYD/DDB)
nodes.rationale: Year-by-year depreciation/book value driving the tax shields in capex NPV/IRR.

nodes.label: Hallazgo estructural: Kern es capa de decision, no ejecucion transaccional
nodes.rationale: Patron repetido en las 8 zonas sin excepcion: ningun sombrero tiene cubierta la ejecucion transaccional en vivo (PO issuance, MRP/explosion de BOM, WMS/pick-path, TMS/tracking, MES/ordenes de trabajo, CAPA/QMS documental). Confirma con evidencia sistematica (29 roles, 8 zonas) el descarte previo del posicionamiento 'Kern como 5PL': Kern decide, no ejecuta; el posicionamiento correcto es la capa de decision que se sienta arriba de quien ejecuta.
nodes.norm_label: hallazgo estructural: kern es capa de decision, no ejecucion transaccional

nodes.label: Propuesta de modo 'lente' (hat) en scm_agent/modes.py
nodes.rationale: modes.py hoy solo distingue Inventory vs SCM (particion por dominio de datos, no por quien pregunta). Se propone una tercera dimension: una lente que el orquestador adopta al enmarcar la respuesta (p.ej. lente CSCO prioriza risk/sop/gobernanza de autonomia, citando el temario de una certificacion como marco de referencia, nunca como certificacion de Kern), definiendo que capacidades priorizar, que KPIs encabezan el resumen, y el nivel de auto-ejecucion vs escalamiento a HANDOFF via guided.py segun el peso de la decision del rol. Riesgo a vigilar: que el usuario confunda la lente con una acreditacion real.

nodes.label: Proximo paso propuesto: nuevas capacidades desde gaps del atlas
nodes.rationale: Usar los gaps repetidos en varios sombreros (no en uno solo) como senal para nuevas capacidades: (a) sostenibilidad/emisiones (Return, CPO, Distribution, CSCO) ya es evolucion E1; (b) SRM/gestion de proveedor profunda (Category Manager, Sourcing Manager, CPO) ya es E2; (c) trazabilidad/genealogia (Quality Manager, Trade Compliance) ya es E3. El atlas no encontro ningun gap de alta senal no cubierto ya por E1-E5, validando esas propuestas con evidencia nueva en vez de requerir una sexta evolucion.
nodes.norm_label: proximo paso propuesto: nuevas capacidades desde gaps del atlas
